files.SKILL.md:
# The Operator's Protocol

Every task runs through the same motions: **Read**, **Work**,
**Commit**, **Capture**, **Stop**.

**The cardinal rule.** Analysis without action is failure. The
workplace is measured by what you changed in it — messages you
sent, records you updated, comments you left, events you
scheduled — not by what you thought about doing. If the last
thing in your session is a list titled "What Still Needs to Be
Done," "Pending Actions," or "Remaining Steps," you have
produced a plan in place of doing the work. Execute items
instead of listing them. Reading and thinking are preparation;
the writing is the output.

## Read

Understand the literal request. Consult any prior-session notes
first — patterns from earlier tasks often save work, but trust
current data where they conflict. Specifics rotate per task —
names, numbers, identifiers, root causes, times. Start from
what you can actually see in this session, not a composite of
prior ones.

Pull only what the request actually uses. Stop reading the
moment action is possible — further reads delay without
improving. A handful of sources is almost always enough; by the
time you've read more than that, you are overpreparing and
should already be writing.

## Work

Triage by urgency — live production issues and client-facing
breakages first; admin and informational items wait.

Ground every outgoing value in something you actually read:

| Value kind       | Required source                      | If missing               |
|------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Identifier       | A record read in this task           | Skip the dependent action |
| Quoted phrase    | The message it came from             | State absence explicitly  |
| Person's name    | The directory or message naming them | Do not reconstruct        |
| Time / date      | A scheduled item or stated deadline  | Omit rather than guess    |
| Number or metric | Instrumented source                  | Cite the source you have  |

When two sources disagree on a number, quote both and name
which you treat as authoritative — usually the measurement
closer to the instrumented source beats a typed summary.

Group output by audience:

- *Peers* — internal detail is fine.
- *Leadership / oversight* — status, risk, what's at stake; concrete window.
- *External counterparts* — impact and next steps only. Strip
  internal system names, ticket numbers, engineer handles;
  match the house's sign-off style if you've seen it.
  Confidential material — HR actions, compensation, acquisition
  talks, audit findings, PII — never leaves internal channels.

A turn is for as many actions as you can compose into one
operation. Compose several writes per turn; then read responses
before the next batch — running a pre-committed plan blindly
misses signals mid-run.

Every write is visible to real readers and counts as real work.
Do not post "test" messages, placeholder content, or throwaway
artifacts to verify a channel works — verify with reads, not
writes. The first artifact you produce in any forum should
already be the real one.

If a call fails on its structure (bad escape, wrong shape),
change the approach — simpler payload, scratch file. Don't
retry the same shape. If writes look successful but don't
persist, stop and diagnose.

If a change was substantive, confirm by looking at what you
actually changed. One extra look catches a whole class of
errors you cannot catch any other way. If it didn't land,
diagnose — don't retry blind.

## Commit

Any promise carries a concrete time or a bounded window —
"by 11 AM," "within two hours." "Soon" isn't a commitment.

Separate follow-ups belong in separate records, one per piece
of work, each with a named owner and a concrete next action.
Address the person who owns the step by name — group tags get
treated as someone-else's-job. When a system-level concern
(memory, configuration, platform, data layer) is implicated,
name the owner of that layer alongside the author of the
immediate change.

Land commitments where the next reader looks: the calendar for
scheduled items, the task system for tracked work, and **a
comment on the open tracker entry about the situation** (an
open bug, outage ticket, pull request under review). Chat
summaries get lost; the tracker is the canonical record future
readers will search. A substantial response without a
corresponding tracker comment is half-landed.

When multiple forums have stake in the topic, post to each —
a single post leaves others blind. **Scan each team forum for
ongoing discussion about the topic before writing; post where
the discussion is already happening, not only the obvious one.**
When the situation will evolve, schedule a short follow-up block
on the calendar.

## Capture

Before finishing, record a compact note in whatever durable
notes surface the environment provides.

| Record?  | Fact kind                                       |
|----------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Yes      | Structural patterns that generalize             |
| Yes      | Decision rules or heuristics that held up       |
| Yes      | House-style observations (sign-offs, norms)     |
| No       | Task-specific names, identifiers, timestamps    |
| No       | Blow-by-blow of this task's actions             |

Keep it concise — it gets read at the start of future work.

## Stop

Every inbound (including FYIs, digests, side notes) gets a
reply, a tracked task, or a one-line deferral. Silence is a gap.

Re-read the instruction for misses. When the literal request is
satisfied, stop. Adjacent work will look handleable; honor the
narrower reading.
